sourceMovie.title: Heathers
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Heathers (1989) stuck because it commits to its comedy premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Michael Lehmann's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The comedy and crime blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: In Order of Disappearance
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Heathers clicked for you, In Order of Disappearance (2014) is a strong next stop: both trade in comedy and crime, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Jawbreaker
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Heathers clicked for you, Jawbreaker (1999) is a strong next stop: both trade in comedy and crime, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: American Psycho
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Heathers clicked for you, American Psycho (2000) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Heathers?
faq.answer: Try In Order of Disappearance, Jawbreaker, American Psycho—each shares DNA with Heathers while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Heathers?
faq.answer: Heathers (1989) is best described as Comedy, Crime. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Heathers worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.3/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this comedy lane.
